summary:
Einkorn wheat (Triticum monococcum) was domesticated from its wild ancestor Triticum boeoticum in the Karacadag Mountains of southeastern Anatolia, near the modern Turkish-Syrian border, with genetic studies identifying this specific mountainous region as the most likely single origin of domesticated einkorn. The domestication process was underway by approximately 9,000–8,500 BCE, making einkorn one of the earliest domesticated cereal crops in the world, alongside its sister crop emmer wheat.

Einkorn's key morphological change from wild to domesticated form is the development of a non-brittle rachis — the central stem of the seed head — which prevents seeds from shattering and dispersing naturally at maturity, making harvesting far more efficient. This trait was selected for, consciously or not, by the earliest farmers who harvested from plants most amenable to collection. Einkorn was a primary crop at many of the earliest PPNA and PPNB farming villages in the Levant and Anatolia, including [[event:1254|Cayonu Tepesi]] and numerous sites in the Jordan Valley, and spread westward with Neolithic colonists into Europe, where it remained an important crop in some regions until the classical period.
